passage:
While researching a topic, a student has taken the following notes:
- Redlining was a practice in which banks and the federal government denied mortgages and insurance to residents of predominantly Black neighborhoods from the 1930s through the 1960s.
- The practice prevented Black families from building wealth through homeownership during a period of rapid suburban growth.
- A 2018 study found that formerly redlined neighborhoods still have lower home values and higher poverty rates than comparable non-redlined areas.
- Environmental researchers have found that formerly redlined neighborhoods also have fewer trees and higher average summer temperatures than surrounding areas.

passageMetadata.source: Student research notes on the lasting effects of redlining

stem: The student wants to demonstrate that redlining's effects persist across multiple dimensions today. Which choice most effectively uses relevant information from the notes to accomplish this goal?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Redlining denied mortgages to residents of Black neighborhoods from the 1930s through the 1960s, preventing families from building wealth through homeownership.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Formerly redlined neighborhoods still have lower home values and higher poverty rates, and environmental researchers have found they also have fewer trees and higher summer temperatures.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A 2018 study revealed that neighborhoods targeted by redlining continue to have lower property values and higher poverty rates than comparable areas.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Environmental researchers have discovered that formerly redlined neighborhoods have fewer trees and experience higher average summer temperatures than surrounding areas.

explanation: Choice B is correct because it presents effects across multiple dimensions: economic (lower home values, higher poverty) and environmental (fewer trees, higher temperatures), directly demonstrating that redlining's impact persists in more than one area. Choices C and D each describe only one dimension of the lasting effects, and A focuses on the historical practice itself.

distractorRationale.A: This choice describes the original practice and its immediate economic mechanism but does not address whether the effects persist today.
distractorRationale.C: This choice addresses only the economic dimension (home values and poverty rates) and does not demonstrate effects across multiple dimensions.
distractorRationale.D: This choice covers only the environmental dimension (trees and temperatures) without including the economic effects, failing to show multiple dimensions.
